1	# POLARIS — Security / Privacy Threat Model
2	
3	**Plan V4 item:** S4 — Security / Privacy Threat Model
4	**Audience:** Independent Telus code reviewer
5	**Scope:** `src/polaris_graph` deep-research pipeline + `src/polaris_v6/api` FastAPI surface
6	**Method:** Evidence-based static review. Every claim below cites a file/function/line actually read during this pass. No runtime code was modified.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 0. Executive summary
11	
12	| # | Axis | Severity of residual risk | One-line verdict |
13	|---|------|---------------------------|------------------|
14	| 1 | API authentication | **Low–Med** | Production surface (v6) is JWT-gated by a global dependency with a fail-loud startup check; a **separate legacy `src/auth/` HMAC system with insecure defaults exists but is not wired into the production app**. |
15	| 2 | Crawler SSRF | **CRITICAL (UNRESOLVED)** | Arbitrary URLs from search results and LLM-harvested seeds are fetched verbatim with `follow_redirects=True` and **no private-IP / metadata-address filtering anywhere in the fetch path**. |
16	| 3 | Prompt injection | **Medium** | Real defense exists (per-call random delimiters + evidence-text sanitization, §9.1.7), but coverage is per-call-site, not a single choke point; no output-side validation of injected instructions. |
17	| 4 | PII | **Medium** | Query + document + retrieved text are sent verbatim to third-party providers (OpenRouter, Serper, etc.). **No PII detection or redaction exists.** The only "scrubber" is a chain-of-thought remover, not a PII remover. |
18	| 5 | Log redaction | **Low** | API keys are not logged (only `bool(present)` / model / budget). A dedicated secret-scanner (`scripts/autoloop/scan_for_secrets.py`) guards commits. |
19	| 6 | Checkpoint retention | **Medium** | LangGraph SQLite checkpoints persist full pipeline state (including retrieved third-party content and drafts) at rest with **no automated retention/TTL/cleanup policy**; deletion is manual, per-vector only. |
20	
21	**UNRESOLVED CRITICAL:** Axis 2 (Crawler SSRF). See §2.
22	
23	---
24	
25	## 1. API authentication
26	
27	### The production surface
28	
29	The ASGI app the deployment serves is built by `create_app()` in
30	`src/polaris_v6/api/app.py:71`. Authentication is a **global** FastAPI
31	dependency applied to every route:
32	
33	```
34	app = FastAPI(..., dependencies=[Depends(_require_auth)])   # app.py:79
35	```
36	
37	`_require_auth` is `require_auth` from `src/polaris_v6/api/auth.py:129`. Design:
38	
39	- **Scheme:** HS256 JWT signed with `POLARIS_JWT_SECRET`
40	  (`auth.py:88-96`, `issue_token` at `auth.py:113`). Login is
41	  `POST /auth/login` against a bcrypt-hashed static-accounts YAML
42	  (`auth.py:167`, `_pwd_ctx = CryptContext(schemes=["bcrypt"])` at `auth.py:50`).
43	- **Fail-loud startup:** `verify_app_startup()` (`auth.py:100`) is called
44	  at app construction (`app.py:73`). It raises `RuntimeError` if
45	  `POLARIS_JWT_SECRET` is missing or `< 32` chars (`auth.py:89-96`) or if
46	  the static-accounts YAML is missing/malformed (`auth.py:73-86`). The app
47	  refuses to boot with a broken auth gate.
48	- **Public allowlist** (`PUBLIC_PATH_PREFIXES`, `auth.py:35-42`):
49	  `/health`, `/transparency`, `/auth/login`, `/docs`, `/redoc`,
50	  `/openapi.json`. All other paths require a valid Bearer token.
51	- **SSE exception:** for `/stream/` paths only, the JWT may arrive as an
52	  `access_token` query param (because browser `EventSource` cannot set
53	  headers) — explicitly gated so lookalike paths and state-changing routes
54	  cannot use it (`auth.py:143-152`).
55	
56	**Deployment secret hygiene:** `POLARIS_JWT_SECRET` is documented as a
57	64-byte URL-safe random value sourced from AWS Secrets Manager via
58	cloud-init, never in git (`docs/carney_secret_inventory.md:17`,
59	`docs/runbook.md:379`).
60	
61	### The auth-disabled trap door
62	
63	`require_auth` short-circuits to `None` (auth OFF) when
64	`POLARIS_AUTH_DISABLED=1` (`auth.py:106-107`), and `verify_app_startup()`
65	also skips its checks under the same flag (`auth.py:104-105`). This is
66	labelled "Phase-0 demo + tests". **Residual risk:** if this flag ever ships
67	enabled to a non-demo environment, the entire API is unauthenticated. It is
68	a single env-var away from open.
69	
70	### Legacy / non-production auth systems (NOT wired into the served app)
71	
72	Two other auth implementations exist in the tree. A reviewer will find them
73	and should be told they are **not** on the production request path:
74	
75	1. **`src/auth/` (HMAC-token system).**
76	   - `AUTH_ENABLED = os.getenv("POLARIS_AUTH_ENABLED","0") == "1"` — **off by
77	     default** (`src/auth/auth_manager.py:16`).
78	   - Hardcoded fallback secret:
79	     `AUTH_SECRET_KEY = os.getenv("POLARIS_AUTH_SECRET","polaris-dev-secret-change-in-production")`
80	     (`auth_manager.py:17`).
81	   - Default admin account created as `admin` / password `admin` if no users
82	     file exists (`_ensure_default_admin`, `auth_manager.py:135-152`;
83	     `POLARIS_ADMIN_PASSWORD` default `"admin"`).
84	   - Passwords hashed as **unsalted-per-user SHA-256** with a single static
85	     global salt (`_hash_password`, `auth_manager.py:155-159`) — fast hash,
86	     no bcrypt, weak against offline cracking.
87	   - **Grep confirms it is not imported by the served app:** no production
88	     module under `src/polaris_v6/` or the pipeline imports
89	     `src/auth/auth_routes` / `auth_manager` / `auth_middleware`.
90	2. **`src/polaris_graph/audit_ir/auth_middleware.py` (API-key + org/workspace
91	   RBAC).** A more rigorous per-resource, cross-tenant-scoped system
92	   (`require_authenticated_caller`, `require_org_member_of`, 403-not-404 to
93	   avoid existence leaks — `auth_middleware.py:262-276`). It has a test-only
94	   `X-Polaris-Caller` header gated behind `PG_AUTH_TRUSTED_TEST_HEADER`
95	   (default off, `auth_middleware.py:122-135`). **However**, the v6 facade
96	   `src/polaris_v6/api/inspector.py:15-19` *deliberately does NOT mount*
97	   `polaris_graph.audit_ir.inspector_router`, so this RBAC layer is also off
98	   the served path in the current demo build.
99	
100	**Reviewer takeaway:** authentication on the shipped surface is coherent and
101	fail-loud. The risk is **three parallel auth systems**, two of which are
102	dormant, one of which (`src/auth/`) carries insecure defaults (default
103	admin/admin, hardcoded dev secret, SHA-256 passwords). If a future wiring
104	change mounts `src/auth/` routes, those defaults become live. Recommend
105	deleting or clearly quarantining the dormant systems.
106	
107	### CORS
108	
109	`create_app()` sets CORS from `POLARIS_V6_CORS_ORIGINS` defaulting to
110	`localhost`/`127.0.0.1` dev ports, with `allow_credentials=False`,
111	`allow_methods=["*"]`, `allow_headers=["*"]` (`app.py:81-92`). With
112	credentials off and an explicit origin allowlist this is acceptable, but
113	the wildcard methods/headers should be tightened for a production origin.
114	
115	---
116	
117	## 2. Crawler SSRF — **UNRESOLVED CRITICAL**
118	
119	### The threat
120	
121	The pipeline (a) issues search queries whose result URLs it then fetches for
122	page content, and (b) lets an LLM "harvest" seed URLs that are fetched
123	**verbatim**. If any of those URLs point at internal infrastructure
124	(`http://169.254.169.254/…` cloud metadata, `http://localhost:…`,
125	`http://10./172.16./192.168.` private ranges, or a redirect that lands
126	there), the server will fetch them and return their bodies into the
127	pipeline — classic Server-Side Request Forgery, leading to cloud-credential
128	theft, internal service enumeration, and internal data exfiltration.
129	
130	### Evidence there is no SSRF control
131	
132	I grepped the entire `src/` tree for the standard SSRF guards
133	(`is_private`, `ip_address`, `169.254`, `is_loopback`, `is_link_local`,
134	`gethostbyname`, `socket.getaddrinfo`, `ssrf`). **Zero of these appear in
135	any fetch path.** The only `urlparse(...).hostname` uses that exist are for
136	domain de-duplication and authority scoring, e.g.
137	`src/polaris_graph/generator/weighted_enrichment.py:504`,
138	`src/polaris_graph/synthesis/independence_collapse.py:173`,
139	`src/polaris_graph/retrieval/evidence_selector.py:1286` — none validate the
140	host against a blocklist before fetching.
141	
142	### The vulnerable fetch code
143	
144	- **Naive/fallback fetcher:** `_fetch_content_httpx_naive(url, max_chars)` in
145	  `src/polaris_graph/retrieval/live_retriever.py:2999` does
146	  `httpx.Client(timeout=..., follow_redirects=True, headers={...}).get(url)`
147	  (`live_retriever.py:3012-3025`) on a caller-supplied `url` with **no host
148	  validation** and **redirect-following enabled** — a redirect to
149	  `169.254.169.254` is followed.
150	- **Additional redirect-following fetchers with no host check:**
151	  `src/polaris_graph/retrieval/domain_backends.py:211,246`,
152	  `src/utils/ingest.py:1959`,
153	  `src/tools/core_client.py:215`, and the DOI resolver
154	  `src/tools/access_bypass.py:5590`
155	  (`aiohttp … session.get(url, allow_redirects=True)`).
156	- **LLM-controlled seed URLs:** `src/polaris_graph/retrieval/agentic_url_harvester.py:8`
157	  documents that harvested candidate URLs are *"fetched **verbatim** by
158	  `live_retriever.run_live_retrieval(seed_urls=…, seed_only=True)`"*
159	  (also lines 44, 157). This means a prompt-injection payload embedded in a
160	  fetched page can name an internal URL that the pipeline will then fetch —
161	  compounding axis 3 into an SSRF primitive.
162	
163	### Current mitigation
164	
165	Partial and incidental only:
166	- `real_fetcher.py` (Serper/Semantic-Scholar) hits **fixed API endpoints**
167	  (`SERPER_ENDPOINT`, `S2_ENDPOINT`, `real_fetcher.py:41-43`), so the
168	  *search* step itself is not SSRF-exposed — but the *content-fetch* step
169	  that follows the returned URLs is.
170	- `access_bypass.py` has a domain **allowlist of low-quality Q&A sites to
171	  drop** (`access_bypass.py:154`) — that is a content-quality filter, not a
172	  security control; it does not block internal hosts.
173	- Fetches are timeout-bounded and there is a `fetch_limiter`
174	  (`src/polaris_graph/retrieval/fetch_limiter.py`), which caps concurrency but
175	  not destination.
176	
177	### Residual risk: **CRITICAL**
178	
179	No allow/deny list on destination host, no DNS-resolution-then-IP check, no
180	private-range block, no redirect-target revalidation. On any cloud host with
181	a metadata service, a single attacker-controlled URL (planted in a web page
182	the pipeline fetches, or a poisoned search result) can exfiltrate instance
183	credentials.
184	
185	**Recommended remediation (for the reviewer to require):** a single
186	choke-point `validate_fetch_target(url)` applied to *every* outbound fetch:
187	resolve the hostname, reject any resolved IP in private/loopback/link-local/
188	metadata ranges, reject non-http(s) schemes, and re-validate after each
189	redirect hop (or disable redirects and validate each hop manually). Until
190	this exists, treat the crawler as SSRF-exploitable.
191	
192	---
193	
194	## 3. Prompt injection
195	
196	### The threat
197	
198	Retrieved web pages, PDFs, and uploaded documents are untrusted text placed
199	inside LLM prompts. A page can contain "ignore previous instructions…" style
200	payloads that hijack the generator/classifier.
201	
202	### Current mitigation (real, but per-call-site)
203	
204	There is a genuine, named defense — "§9.1.7 delimiter/injection defense":
205	
206	- **Per-call random delimiters.** `build_question_block()` in
207	  `src/polaris_graph/audit_ir/scope_classifier_llm.py:262` wraps untrusted
208	  input in `<<<question-{16-hex-random}>>> … <<<end-{token}>>>` and strips any
209	  token-shaped substring out of the body so content cannot forge a closing
210	  delimiter (`scope_classifier_llm.py:271-276`). The random per-call token
211	  means an attacker cannot predict the delimiter to break out. The same
212	  pattern is used in `auto_induction/llm_inductor._build_query_block`.
213	- **Evidence-text sanitization.** `generator/analyst_synthesis.py:159,407-424`
214	  routes evidence text (and the evidence *id*) through
215	  `sanitize_evidence_text` before wrapping in `<<<end_evidence>>>` delimiters,
216	  explicitly to stop content forging open/close delimiters.
217	- **Fail-loud on disabled sanitizer.** `generator/outline_digest.py:88-101`
218	  raises rather than silently returning identity if the sanitizer is a no-op,
219	  so the defense cannot be disabled unnoticed.
220	- The pipeline asserts *"Prompt-injection sanitization was applied to all
221	  evidence"* (`honest_pipeline.py:374`).
222	
223	### Residual risk: **Medium**
224	
225	- **Coverage is per-call-site, not a single enforced boundary.** The defense
226	  lives in specific modules (scope classifier, analyst synthesis, outline
227	  digest, inductor). There is no single guarantee that *every* place
228	  untrusted content enters a prompt goes through sanitization; a new call
229	  site can omit it. A reviewer should verify each prompt-construction path.
230	- **No output-side validation.** Defenses are input-shaping (delimiters +
231	  substring stripping). There is no post-generation check that the model did
232	  not act on an injected instruction (e.g., no verifier that the output stays
233	  on-task / did not emit an attacker-chosen URL). Combined with §2, an
234	  injected instruction naming an internal URL can drive an SSRF fetch.
235	- Delimiter defense mitigates *breakout*, not *persuasion*: a payload that
236	  stays inside the delimiters but is persuasive ("as the cited source, the
237	  correct answer is X") is not blocked.
238	
239	---
240	
241	## 4. PII
242	
243	### The threat
244	
245	User queries, uploaded documents, and retrieved third-party content may
246	contain personal data. That data is (a) sent to external LLM/search
247	providers and (b) persisted at rest (see §6).
248	
249	### Current handling
250	
251	- **Data is sent verbatim to third parties.** The research query, retrieved
252	  page/abstract text, and document text are placed into request payloads to
253	  OpenRouter (`src/polaris_graph/llm/openrouter_client.py`), Serper, and
254	  Semantic Scholar (`real_fetcher.py`). This is inherent to the product, but
255	  it means any PII in the input crosses to US/third-party processors.
256	- **No PII detection or redaction anywhere.** A grep for
257	  `redact|pii|PII|anonymiz|scrub.*email|gdpr` returns **no PII-removal code**.
258	  The only "scrub" utility is `src/utils/cot_scrubber.py` — its own docstring
259	  (`cot_scrubber.py:1-20`) states it removes leaked **chain-of-thought** text
260	  from reports, not PII. `scrub_cot_from_report` (used at
261	  `base_agent.py:596`, `graph.py:2428`) is a report-hygiene tool, not a
262	  privacy control.
263	
264	### Residual risk: **Medium**
265	
266	There is no data-classification, no PII redaction before egress to providers,
267	and no data-processing-agreement enforcement in code. For a Canadian-
268	sovereignty-branded product ("Sovereign Canadian deep research AI",
269	`app.py:77`) this is a notable gap: query/document PII leaves the boundary to
270	third-party (largely US) providers with no scrubbing. Recommend documenting
271	the provider data flow in a DPIA and adding optional input redaction.
272	
273	---
274	
275	## 5. Log redaction
276	
277	### The threat
278	
279	Secrets (`OPENROUTER_API_KEY`, `SERPER_API_KEY`, JWT secret, etc.) leaking
280	into logs or committed transcripts.
281	
282	### Current handling — good
283	
284	- **Keys are not logged as values.** The OpenRouter client logs only
285	  `model` and `budget` at init (`openrouter_client.py:1563-1567`); the key is
286	  placed solely into the `Authorization: Bearer` header
287	  (`openrouter_client.py:1587`), never into a log statement. The search agent
288	  logs only **presence** — `f"... API key present: {bool(api_key)}"`
289	  (`agents/search_agent.py:492`) — not the value.
290	- **TLS verification stays on.** The client shares a cert-verifying
291	  `SSLContext` (`openrouter_client.py:1583`, `get_shared_ssl_context`), so
292	  bearer tokens are not sent over unverified TLS.
293	- **Dedicated secret scanner.** `scripts/autoloop/scan_for_secrets.py`
294	  carries regexes for OpenAI/Slack/Google/Stripe keys, PEM private keys,
295	  `Authorization: Bearer` tokens, basic-auth URLs, and a vendor-agnostic
296	  `configured_secret_assignment` pattern that matches
297	  `POLARIS_JWT_SECRET`/`POLARIS_AUTH_SECRET`/vendor keys followed by a
298	  key-shaped value (`scan_for_secrets.py:40-71`). Matches are reported with
299	  the value redacted to an 8-char prefix (`scan_for_secrets.py:81`). A
300	  `pre-commit` config exists (`.pre-commit-config.yaml`).
301	- Secrets are documented as env-injected from AWS Secrets Manager, never
302	  committed (`docs/carney_secret_inventory.md`, `.env` is gitignored).
303	
304	### Residual risk: **Low**
305	
306	Two things to note rather than genuine holes:
307	- The scanner is a **best-effort regex**, not exhaustive — a novel key shape
308	  (e.g. a future provider) is caught only by the name-based
309	  `configured_secret_assignment` pattern, and only if the env var name is in
310	  its hardcoded list (`scan_for_secrets.py:63-68`).
311	- Third-party libraries (`httpx` at DEBUG, uvicorn access logs) could echo
312	  full URLs. The pipeline's own fetch URLs are truncated in logs
313	  (e.g. `url[:80]` at `live_retriever.py:2985`), but query strings on some
314	  API URLs may carry the S2/NCBI key as a param — worth confirming no fetch
315	  logs the full parameterized URL at INFO. Not observed leaking, but not
316	  provably absent.
317	
318	---
319	
320	## 6. Checkpoint retention
321	
322	### The threat
323	
324	The generation/pipeline checkpoint persists full pipeline state — including
325	the query, retrieved third-party content, and in-progress report drafts — to
326	disk. Sensitive data at rest with no lifecycle is a privacy and data-
327	retention exposure.
328	
329	### Current handling
330	
331	- **Store:** LangGraph `AsyncSqliteSaver` at
332	  `PG_CHECKPOINT_DIR/pg_checkpoints.sqlite`
333	  (`src/polaris_graph/checkpoint_manager.py:29-30`, `get_checkpointer` at
334	  line 37). Gated behind `PG_CHECKPOINT_ENABLED == "1"`
335	  (`checkpoint_manager.py:26`).
336	- **Contents are the full state snapshot.** `get_checkpoint_state()`
337	  returns the *complete* state values at a checkpoint
338	  (`checkpoint_manager.py:244-312`), and `rewind_to_checkpoint`
339	  (line ~337) resumes from them — i.e. drafts and retrieved evidence are
340	  stored verbatim.
341	- **Deletion is manual and per-vector only.** The only cleanup is
342	  `clear_checkpoint(vector_id)` (`checkpoint_manager.py:80-112`), which
343	  issues `DELETE FROM checkpoints/writes WHERE thread_id = ?` for one vector.
344	  There is **no TTL, no age-based prune, no purge job.** A grep for
345	  `retention|cleanup|ttl|expire|prune|purge|max_age` in the checkpoint
346	  manager returns nothing.
347	- No encryption-at-rest is applied by the application layer; the SQLite file
348	  is plaintext on the host volume.
349	
350	### Residual risk: **Medium**
351	
352	Checkpoints accumulate indefinitely unless an operator manually clears each
353	vector. They hold query text, retrieved (possibly copyrighted/PII-bearing)
354	third-party content, and draft reports, unencrypted at rest. There is no
355	data-retention policy expressed in code and no automated deletion after a run
356	completes. Recommend: an age-based retention sweep, opt-in
357	encryption-at-rest (or reliance on an encrypted volume, documented), and
358	auto-clear on successful run completion.

366	| F-1 | **Crawler fetches arbitrary/LLM-supplied URLs with `follow_redirects=True` and no private-IP / metadata (169.254.169.254) / loopback filtering — SSRF.** No `is_private`/`ip_address`/`169.254` guard anywhere in the fetch path. | `live_retriever.py:2999-3025`; `domain_backends.py:211,246`; `access_bypass.py:5590`; `utils/ingest.py:1959`; verbatim seed fetch documented `agentic_url_harvester.py:8` | **CRITICAL (unresolved)** |

===retrieval_route===
"""FastAPI HTTP route for the slice 002 clinical retrieval pipeline.

Per `.codex/slices/slice_002/architecture_proposal.md` §"api/retrieval_route".

Mirrors slice 001's `api/intake_route.py` pattern:

    api/intake_route.py     -> POST /api/intake
    api/retrieval_route.py  -> POST /api/retrieval (this module)

The route accepts a ScopeDecision (typically passed straight from the
slice 001 intake response) and returns either an EvidencePool (HTTP 200)
or a structured RetrievalError body (HTTP 400).

Mount into any FastAPI app:

    from fastapi import FastAPI
    from polaris_graph.api.retrieval_route import router as retrieval_router

    app = FastAPI()
    app.include_router(retrieval_router, prefix="/api")

Tests use FastAPI TestClient (no real HTTP server, no real network).
The fetch_fn is injected via FastAPI's dependency-override mechanism;
in production it will be the real Serper + Semantic-Scholar fetcher
shipped in PR 7.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any

from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

from polaris_graph.clinical_retrieval.clinical_retriever import (
    FetchHttpFn,
    process_retrieval,
)
from polaris_graph.clinical_retrieval.evidence_pool import EvidencePool, RetrievalError
from polaris_graph.scope.scope_decision import ScopeDecision

router = APIRouter(tags=["retrieval"])


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dependency: fetch_fn injection point
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def get_fetch_fn() -> FetchHttpFn | None:
    """Returns the active fetch_fn or None.

    None signals process_retrieval() to use its default sentinel, which
    raises and is caught by the orchestrator -> RetrievalError(
    fetch_backend_unavailable). Tests override this dep to inject stubs.
    PR 7 will replace this default with the real Serper + Semantic-Scholar
    fetcher.
    """
    return None


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Request / response models
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

class RetrievalRequest(BaseModel):
    """POST body for /api/retrieval.

    Accepts a ScopeDecision verbatim (as returned by /api/intake).
    Pydantic re-validates the structure on the way in so a malformed
    decision yields HTTP 422 before the orchestrator runs.
    """

    decision: ScopeDecision = Field(
        description="ScopeDecision from slice 001 intake (status=in_scope, scope_class=clinical_*)",
    )


class RetrievalErrorResponse(BaseModel):
    """Body returned with HTTP 400 when retrieval cannot proceed."""

    error: bool = True
    code: str  # 'wrong_status' | 'wrong_scope_class' | 'fetch_backend_unavailable'
    message: str
    decision_id: str | None = None


class RetrievalSuccessResponse(BaseModel):
    """Body returned with HTTP 200 wrapping a successful EvidencePool."""

    error: bool = False
    pool: dict  # EvidencePool.model_dump(mode="json")
    server_time_utc: str


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Routes
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

@router.post(
    "/retrieval",
    response_model=None,  # union response: success-shape or 400-error
)
def post_retrieval(
    req: RetrievalRequest,
    fetch_fn: FetchHttpFn | None = Depends(get_fetch_fn),
) -> RetrievalSuccessResponse | RetrievalErrorResponse:
    """Run a slice 001 ScopeDecision through clinical retrieval.

    Returns EvidencePool (HTTP 200) on success — note that an EvidencePool
    with adequacy.is_adequate=False is still HTTP 200 (the request
    succeeded, the corpus just isn't strong enough for downstream
    generation; UI surfaces the failure_reason). HTTP 400 is reserved
    for structural failures: wrong status, wrong scope class, fetch
    backend down.
    """
    if fetch_fn is None:
        result = process_retrieval(req.decision)
    else:
        result = process_retrieval(req.decision, fetch_fn=fetch_fn)

content.content:
===real_fetcher head===
"""Real Serper + Semantic Scholar fetcher behind FetchHttpFn protocol.

Per `.codex/slices/slice_002/architecture_proposal.md` §"Implementation order"
PR 7 row.

Two backends, fanned out per query:
  1. Serper (https://google.serper.dev/search) — Google-results proxy.
     Requires SERPER_API_KEY env var. Returns up to 10 hits per query.
  2. Semantic Scholar (https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/search)
     — academic-paper graph. SEMANTIC_SCHOLAR_API_KEY optional but recommended.

Results from both backends merge into a single list[FetchResult] handed back
to the orchestrator, which then runs them through clinical_source_registry
to drop anything not on the T1/T2/T3 allowlist.

Fail-loud per LAW II:
- SERPER_API_KEY missing → RuntimeError at construction. No silent skip.
- Both backends down → RuntimeError. No silent empty list.
- Semantic Scholar without key → still runs (1 RPS unauthenticated tier),
  but logs the missing key for ops visibility.

Rate limiting:

#!/bin/bash
# I-carney-003 — install iptables egress lockdown on the host.
# I-carney-008 iter-3 — IPv6 (ip6tables) parity added per Codex P1-2.
#
# Reads /etc/polaris/egress_allowlist.txt and installs rules in BOTH OUTPUT
# (host) and DOCKER-USER (container forwarded traffic) chains so neither
# host nor containers can reach off-allowlist destinations on 80/443.
#
# Both IPv4 (iptables) AND IPv6 (ip6tables) chains are installed in parallel
# because Vexxhost VMs default to dual-stack. An IPv4-only lockdown would
# leak HTTPS over IPv6.
#
# Idempotent: flushes the POLARIS_EGRESS_HOST + POLARIS_EGRESS_DOCKER chains
# before reapplying. Per Codex iter-2 P1-003: covers BOTH host outbound
# AND Docker-bridge-forwarded container traffic.
#
# Run AFTER the first `docker compose build` (build-time hosts in
# config/egress_allowlist.txt are deb.debian.org / pypi.org / etc).

set -eo pipefail

ALLOWLIST_PATH="${POLARIS_EGRESS_ALLOWLIST:-/etc/polaris/egress_allowlist.txt}"
LOG_FILE="${POLARIS_EGRESS_LOG:-/var/log/polaris-egress.log}"

if [ ! -f "$ALLOWLIST_PATH" ]; then
    echo "[egress] ERROR: allowlist file not found at $ALLOWLIST_PATH" >&2
    echo "[egress] copy config/egress_allowlist.txt → $ALLOWLIST_PATH first" >&2
    exit 1
fi

if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "[egress] ERROR: must run as root (uses iptables + ip6tables)" >&2
    exit 1
fi

log() {
    local ts
    ts=$(date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    echo "[$ts] $*" | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"
}

# Resolve allowlist domains to unique A records (IPv4).
resolve_allowlist_v4() {
    local domain ip
    while IFS= read -r domain; do
        # Skip blanks + comments.
        [[ -z "$domain" || "$domain" =~ ^# ]] && continue
        while IFS= read -r ip; do
            [ -n "$ip" ] && echo "$ip"
        done < <(getent ahostsv4 "$domain" 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $1}' | sort -u)

# (Re)create the chain.
    "$ipt" -F "$chain_name" 2>/dev/null || "$ipt" -N "$chain_name"

    # Allow loopback + established + related.
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -o lo -j ACCEPT || true
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT

    # Allow DNS (53), NTP (123).
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -p udp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -p tcp --dport 53 -j ACCEPT
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -p udp --dport 123 -j ACCEPT

    # Allow link-local cloud metadata. 169.254.169.254/32 for IPv4, fe80::/10
    # for IPv6 link-local block. OpenStack on Vexxhost emits cloud-init via
    # the IPv4 metadata endpoint; IPv6 link-local stays permissive for ND/RA.
    if [ "$ipt" = "iptables" ]; then
        "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -d 169.254.169.254/32 -j ACCEPT
    else
        "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -d fe80::/10 -j ACCEPT
    fi

    # Allow allowlisted IPs on 443 + 80.
    while IFS= read -r ip; do
        [ -z "$ip" ] && continue
        "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -d "$ip" -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
        "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -d "$ip" -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
    done <<< "$resolved"

    # Drop everything else on 80/443.
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -p tcp --dport 443 -j LOG --log-prefix "[POLARIS-EGRESS-DROP] "
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -p tcp --dport 443 -j DROP
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -p tcp --dport 80 -j LOG --log-prefix "[POLARIS-EGRESS-DROP] "
    "$ipt" -A "$chain_name" -p tcp --dport 80 -j DROP

    # Wire the chain into the parent if not already done.
    if ! "$ipt" -C "$parent_chain" -j "$chain_name" 2>/dev/null; then
        "$ipt" -I "$parent_chain" 1 -j "$chain_name"
    fi

    log "chain $chain_name ($ipt) installed with $(echo "$resolved" | grep -c -v '^$') allowed IPs"
}

main() {
    log "egress lockdown starting (allowlist=$ALLOWLIST_PATH)"
    local resolved_v4 resolved_v6
    resolved_v4=$(resolve_allowlist_v4)
    resolved_v6=$(resolve_allowlist_v6)
    if [ -z "$resolved_v4" ] && [ -z "$resolved_v6" ]; then
        echo "[egress] ERROR: allowlist resolved to zero IPs (v4 AND v6)" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    if [ -z "$resolved_v4" ]; then
        log "WARN: zero IPv4 resolutions from allowlist — IPv4 lockdown will drop everything"
    fi
    if [ -z "$resolved_v6" ]; then
        log "WARN: zero IPv6 resolutions from allowlist — IPv6 lockdown will drop everything"
    fi

    # ---- IPv4 ----
    install_chain "POLARIS_EGRESS_HOST" "OUTPUT" "$resolved_v4" "iptables"
    if ! iptables -L DOCKER-USER >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        iptables -N DOCKER-USER 2>/dev/null || true
        iptables -I FORWARD 1 -j DOCKER-USER 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    install_chain "POLARIS_EGRESS_DOCKER" "DOCKER-USER" "$resolved_v4" "iptables"

    # ---- IPv6 ----
    # ip6tables must be present (default on Ubuntu 24.04). If not, fail
    # loudly rather than leak.
    if ! command -v ip6tables >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "[egress] ERROR: ip6tables not found; IPv6 leak would occur. Install iptables-persistent or disable IPv6." >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    install_chain "POLARIS_EGRESS_HOST_V6" "OUTPUT" "$resolved_v6" "ip6tables"
    if ! ip6tables -L DOCKER-USER >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        ip6tables -N DOCKER-USER 2>/dev/null || true
        ip6tables -I FORWARD 1 -j DOCKER-USER 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
